exercises.name: Cable one arm twisting seated row
exercises.notes: Setup: Sit on the floor or bench facing a low cable pulley with legs extended or slightly bent and feet braced against a foot platform or the floor; attach a single D‑handle to the low pulley.
instructions: Setup: Sit on the floor or bench facing a low cable pulley with legs extended or slightly bent and feet braced against a foot platform or the floor; attach a single D‑handle to the low pulley.
Grip & posture: Reach forward with one hand and grasp the handle with a neutral or slightly supinated grip; sit tall with a neutral spine, chest up, shoulders down and back, and brace your core.
Starting position: Allow your arm to extend fully in front of you while maintaining an upright torso; keep the opposite hand resting on your thigh or hip for balance.
Pull & twist: Exhale and pull the handle toward your lower ribs/hip by driving your elbow back while simultaneously rotating your torso toward the pulling side so your chest turns toward the cable.
Squeeze: At the end range, actively squeeze the lats and retract the shoulder blade on the working side for a brief 1–2 second pause.
Return: Inhale and slowly control the handle back to the starting position while reversing the twist, keeping tension on the cable and a neutral spine throughout.

Repetitions & safety: Perform the desired reps, then switch sides; use a weight you can control without using momentum, avoid excessive lumbar rotation, and maintain core bracing to protect the spine.
exercises.description: Seated one-arm twisting cable row is a unilateral back exercise where you sit facing the low pulley and pull a single handle toward your torso while rotating the trunk, emphasizing the latissimus and mid-back. The twisting component increases core stabilization and helps target the lats and scapular retractors through a larger range of motion.

exercises.name: Cable Seated Single Arm Row
exercises.notes: Set a single-hand D-handle on the low or mid pulley of a cable machine and select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Set a single-hand D-handle on the low or mid pulley of a cable machine and select an appropriate weight.
Sit on a flat bench perpendicular to the cable column so the working side faces the machine; feet flat on the floor, knees bent and stable.
Reach forward with the working arm and grab the handle with a neutral grip (palm facing in), arm extended and torso upright — this is the start position.
Brace your core, keep your chest up and spine neutral, and minimize torso rotation or leaning back throughout the movement.
Pull the handle toward your torso by driving the elbow back and slightly down, leading with the elbow and squeezing your shoulder blade toward your spine.
Hold a brief peak contraction (1 second) with the lat and upper-back engaged, avoiding shrugging the shoulder toward the ear.
Slowly extend the arm under control back to the start position until it is nearly straight, maintaining tension in the cable.
Repeat for the desired repetitions, then switch sides and perform the same steps with the opposite arm.
exercises.description: A unilateral seated cable row that emphasizes lat activation and scapular retraction while allowing a controlled, full-range single-arm pull. Performed from a seated position beside a cable column to isolate one side and reduce compensatory torso movement.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Palm Rotational Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Keep a neutral spine and neck (eyes toward the floor a few feet in front of you).
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart holding a dumbbell in each hand. Slightly bend the knees and hinge forward at the hips until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor (bent-over position).
Keep a neutral spine and neck (eyes toward the floor a few feet in front of you). Let the dumbbells hang at arm's length beneath your shoulders with a pronated or neutral grip.
Brace your core and initiate the movement by pulling your shoulder blades back and down (scapular retraction).

Pull the dumbbells upward toward your lower ribs/waist by driving the elbows up and back. As you ascend, rotate your wrists so the palms turn (for example from pronation/neutral toward supination) at the top of the row.
Squeeze the upper back and rear delts at the top for a brief pause, keeping elbows close to the body and avoiding excessive torso rotation or momentum.
Lower the dumbbells slowly back to the starting position while rotating the palms back to the initial orientation, maintaining control throughout.
Repeat for the desired reps. Maintain the hip hinge, slight knee bend, and neutral spine for the entire set.
exercises.description: A bent-over dumbbell row variation where the wrists/palms rotate during the pull to increase scapular retraction and arm involvement. Performed in a hip-hinged position, it emphasizes the upper back while also engaging the lats, rear delts and arms.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Low Fly
exercises.notes: Start standing with feet hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees soft and a slight hinge at the hips (standing, slight forward lean).
instructions: Start standing with feet hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees soft and a slight hinge at the hips (standing, slight forward lean).
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral grip at or just above thigh level (low position). Keep the spine neutral and shoulders down.
Maintain a small, fixed bend in the elbows throughout the movement; do not lock the joints.
Exhale and raise the dumbbells in a wide arc up and inward, leading with the elbows so the hands meet or come close in front of the upper chest/shoulder level (low-to-high arc).
Pause briefly at the top and actively squeeze the pectorals while keeping the shoulders stable (avoid shrugging).
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back along the same arc to the starting low position under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, keeping tempo controlled and avoiding momentum or excessive back extension.
exercises.description: A standing low-to-high dumbbell fly that moves the weights in an upward arc from near the hips to in front of the chest, emphasizing the pectorals. Keep a slight bend in the elbows and squeeze the chest at the top of the movement.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie back so you are supine on the bench, feet flat on the floor, knees bent, and maintain a neutral spine with a slight natural arch; retract and squeeze your shoulder blades together.
instructions: Sit on the edge of a flat bench with a dumbbell resting on each thigh.
Lie back so you are supine on the bench, feet flat on the floor, knees bent, and maintain a neutral spine with a slight natural arch; retract and squeeze your shoulder blades together.
Use your legs and core to help bring the dumbbells up to chest height so your upper arms are about 45 degrees from your torso and palms are facing forward (or slightly inward if preferred).

Inhale, then slowly lower the dumbbells in a controlled manner until your elbows are roughly level with the bench or just below chest level, keeping wrists neutral and elbows tracking slightly tucked.

Pause briefly at the bottom without relaxing the shoulder blade position, then exhale and press the dumbbells upward and slightly together until your arms are almost fully extended — avoid locking the elbows.
Control the descent back to the starting position and repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
To finish the set, bring the dumbbells safely to your knees, sit up, and set them down or have a spotter assist if using heavy weight.
exercises.description: The dumbbell bench press is a supine pressing movement performed on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and delts for elbow and shoulder extension. Dumbbells allow a greater range of motion and require more stabilization from the forearms and serratus anterior.

exercises.name: Cable Seated Chest Fly
exercises.notes: Set both cable pulleys to about chest height and select an appropriate weight; sit upright on a bench with feet flat on the floor and the bench back supporting your spine.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to about chest height and select an appropriate weight; sit upright on a bench with feet flat on the floor and the bench back supporting your spine.
Grasp a handle in each hand with a neutral or slight supinated grip and take one step forward so there is tension in the cables; arms should start out to the sides with a small bend in the elbows.
Retract and depress your shoulder blades slightly and keep your chest up; maintain a stable, upright seated position throughout the movement.
Exhale as you draw both handles together in a wide arc in front of your chest, keeping the elbows slightly bent and fixed in that angle; aim to have the hands meet or come close at midline.
Pause briefly at the peak contraction and actively squeeze the pectorals without shrugging the shoulders or stepping forward with the torso.
Inhale and slowly return the handles back to the starting position in a controlled manner, allowing the chest to stretch but keeping tension on the cables.
Keep the movement driven by the chest—avoid using momentum, excessive elbow extension, or large torso lean; if balance is an issue, press your back into the bench.
Perform the desired number of reps with consistent form, resetting scapular position between reps if needed and adjusting weight if you cannot maintain control.
exercises.description: Seated cable chest fly is an isolation movement performed on dual cable pulleys, bringing the arms together in a horizontal arc to emphasize the pectorals. It uses a slight elbow bend and controlled tempo to maximize chest contraction while seated upright.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Every working set should reach true muscular failure.

exercises.notes: Light weight, focus on form and range of motion

exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet hip-width apart, knees slightly bent, and core braced (standing position).

exercises.notes: Stand upright with feet hip-width apart, knees soft, and core braced to maintain a neutral spine.

workout.title: Shoulders/Arms - BODYBUILD/ENDURANCE
workout.description: Shoulders/Arms workout focusing on bodybuild and endurance training.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Overhead Press
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet hip-width apart, knees slightly bent, and core braced (standing position).
instructions: Stand tall with feet hip-width apart, knees slightly bent, and core braced (standing position).
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height with palms facing forward or neutral and elbows slightly in front of the body.
Keep the spine neutral and ribcage down to avoid hyperextending the lower back; squeeze the abs throughout.
Exhale and press both dumbbells upward in a controlled path until the arms are nearly fully extended overhead without locking the elbows.
Pause briefly at the top with shoulders stacked over hips and the head neutral (you may slightly move your head back to allow the dumbbells to pass), keeping shoulder blades stable.
Inhale and lower the dumbbells slowly back to the starting shoulder-height position under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, avoiding excessive torso lean or lower-back arching; if needed, use a lighter weight to maintain good form.
exercises.description: A standing dumbbell overhead press is a compound shoulder exercise where you press dumbbells from shoulder height to full arm extension overhead, primarily working the delts while engaging the triceps and upper back for support and stabilization.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Arnold Press
exercises.notes: Stand upright with feet hip-width apart, knees soft, and core braced to maintain a neutral spine.
instructions: Stand upright with feet hip-width apart, knees soft, and core braced to maintain a neutral spine.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height with elbows bent and palms facing your torso (palms-in, front-rack position).
Initiate the movement by rotating your wrists outward as you begin to press the dumbbells upward, so palms face forward at the top of the movement.
Press the weights overhead until your arms are nearly straight (avoid locking the elbows), keeping control and a straight line from wrists to elbows.
Pause briefly at the top with palms facing forward, shoulders engaged but not shrugged.
Slowly lower the dumbbells back down while rotating the wrists inward so palms return to facing your torso and the dumbbells return to the starting position at shoulder height.
Maintain an upright torso and braced core throughout; avoid excessive arching of the lower back or using leg drive to lift the weights.
Breathe out while pressing up and inhale while lowering. Perform controlled repetitions with full rotation on each rep.
exercises.description: A standing dumbbell Arnold press is a rotating overhead press that targets the delts while involving the triceps and upper back for support. It begins with palms facing the body at shoulder level and rotates the wrists as you press overhead to emphasize the front and side deltoid heads.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Rear Lateral Raise (support head)
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench so the pad is angled and stand facing the bench; hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees and rest your forehead (or chest/upper stomach) lightly against the bench pad to support the head and torso.
instructions: Set an incline bench so the pad is angled and stand facing the bench; hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees and rest your forehead (or chest/upper stomach) lightly against the bench pad to support the head and torso.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral grip (palms facing each other) and let the arms hang straight down beneath your shoulders; maintain a neutral spine and brace your abs.
Begin the movement by initiating at the shoulders: raise both dumbbells out to the sides in a wide arc with a slight bend in the elbows, leading with the elbows rather than the hands.
Continue lifting until your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor (or in line with your shoulders), keeping the wrists neutral and avoiding excessive elbow lock.
Pause and squeeze the rear delts/upper back at the top for 1 second while keeping the head and torso supported on the bench.
Slowly lower the dumbbells back to the start position under control, resisting any tendency to let momentum drop the weights.
Breathe out as you lift and inhale as you lower. Maintain a stable torso—do not rotate, jerk, or shrug the shoulders during the movement.
Use lighter weight if you cannot perform the motion with controlled form or if you feel neck or lumbar strain despite the head support.
exercises.description: A bent-over rear lateral raise performed with the head supported on an incline bench to isolate the posterior delts; lift dumbbells out to the sides while keeping the torso stable to target the rear shoulder and upper back. This variation reduces momentum and neck strain by using the bench for support.

exercises.name: Band Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Stand upright with feet together or hip-width apart, step on the middle of the band to anchor it securely under both feet.
instructions: Stand upright with feet together or hip-width apart, step on the middle of the band to anchor it securely under both feet.
Hold a handle in each hand with palms facing your body and arms hanging at your sides; keep a slight bend in the elbows.
Brace your abs and keep your torso tall and still; avoid leaning or using momentum.
Exhale and lift both arms out to the sides leading with the elbows until your hands reach shoulder height (arms roughly parallel to the floor).
Keep wrists neutral and maintain the slight elbow bend throughout the movement; do not shrug the shoulders upward.
Pause briefly at the top to feel the delts, then inhale and slowly lower the arms back to the starting position under control.
Repeat for desired reps, maintaining smooth, controlled movement and stable posture on every repetition.
exercises.description: Standing lateral raise using a resistance band where you lift the arms out to the sides to shoulder height to emphasize the lateral delts. The band provides continuous tension while the traps and forearms assist for stability and grip.

exercises.name: Cable Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single handles.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single handles. Stand centered between the two towers and grasp each handle with the opposite hand so the cables cross in front of you (or hold same-side handles if preferred).
Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart, knees soft, and core braced (neutral spine). Keep a slight bend in the elbows and wrists neutral.
Start with the handles low by your thighs; palms can face inward or slightly forward. Maintain a small forward lean from the hips if comfortable to allow a natural arc.
Keeping the elbows slightly bent, exhale and raise both arms out to the sides in a smooth arc until your hands reach approximately shoulder height. Lead the movement with the elbow, not the wrist.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining tension in the delts; avoid shrugging the shoulders upward toward your ears.
Inhale and lower the handles slowly and under control back to the starting position, keeping tension on the cables throughout the descent.
Repeat for the desired number of reps. Use a lighter weight if you cannot maintain strict form or if you begin using torso momentum.
exercises.description: A standing cable lateral raise performed from low pulleys to isolate the lateral head of the delts; cables provide constant tension while you lift the arms out to the sides to shoulder height. The movement is done with a slight elbow bend and controlled tempo to minimize momentum.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Overhead Press
exercises.notes: Light weight, focus on form and range of motion

instructions: Stand tall with feet hip-width apart, knees slightly bent, and core braced (standing position).
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height with palms facing forward or neutral and elbows slightly in front of the body.
Keep the spine neutral and ribcage down to avoid hyperextending the lower back; squeeze the abs throughout.
Exhale and press both dumbbells upward in a controlled path until the arms are nearly fully extended overhead without locking the elbows.
Pause briefly at the top with shoulders stacked over hips and the head neutral (you may slightly move your head back to allow the dumbbells to pass), keeping shoulder blades stable.
Inhale and lower the dumbbells slowly back to the starting shoulder-height position under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, avoiding excessive torso lean or lower-back arching; if needed, use a lighter weight to maintain good form.
exercises.description: A standing dumbbell overhead press is a compound shoulder exercise where you press dumbbells from shoulder height to full arm extension overhead, primarily working the delts while engaging the triceps and upper back for support and stabilization.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Upright Row
exercises.notes: Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart, knees soft and core braced (neutral spine).
instructions: Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart, knees soft and core braced (neutral spine).
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with an overhand (pronated) grip, arms extended down in front of you and weights close to your thighs.
Initiate the movement by driving your elbows upward and outward, keeping the dumbbells close to the body and leading with the elbows.
Raise the dumbbells until your elbows reach roughly chest/shoulder height or just below chin level, keeping wrists relatively neutral and palms facing your body or slightly inward.
Pause briefly at the top with shoulders engaged but avoid excessive shrugging or leaning back.
Lower the dumbbells slowly and under control back to the starting position, fully extending the arms without locking the elbows.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions while maintaining an upright standing position, steady breathing, and avoiding momentum or swinging.
exercises.description: A standing dumbbell upright row where you pull the weights vertically toward your upper chest, emphasizing the shoulder (deltoid) muscles with assistance from the trapezius, biceps, and forearms. Perform with a controlled motion and elbows leading to keep tension on the delts.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and knees bent at about 90 degrees.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and knees bent at about 90 degrees.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with a neutral grip (palms facing inward), arms fully relaxed.
Brace your core and press your lower back lightly into the bench to maintain a neutral spine throughout.
Slightly bend your elbows (soft arms) and keep the bend constant for the entire movement.
Exhale and raise both arms out to the sides in a controlled arc until your arms are roughly parallel to the floor (shoulder height); lead with the elbows and keep wrists neutral.
Keep shoulders down (do not shrug) and avoid leaning or using torso momentum—if you need to swing, reduce the weight.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining tension in the delts, then inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position.
Perform repetitions with a steady tempo (e.g., 1–2 seconds up, 1–2 seconds down) and choose a weight that allows strict form.
exercises.description: A seated lateral raise isolates the lateral head of the deltoid by lifting light-to-moderate dumbbells out to the sides from a seated, supported position. Use a slight elbow bend and controlled tempo to emphasize shoulder abduction and limit momentum.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Rear Delt Raise
exercises.notes: Stand feet hip-width apart holding a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral (palms facing) grip.
instructions: Stand feet hip-width apart holding a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral (palms facing) grip.
Hinge forward at the hips until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor, keep a neutral spine and knees slightly bent (bent-over position).
Let your arms hang straight down from the shoulders with a slight bend in the elbows; maintain a braced core and avoid rounding the back.
Keeping the elbows slightly bent, raise the dumbbells out to the sides in a wide arc until your upper arms are roughly parallel to the ground (level with the shoulders).
Squeeze the rear delts and shoulder blades together at the top for a brief pause, avoid shrugging the shoulders up toward the ears.
Slowly lower the dumbbells back to the start under control, resisting any momentum; repeat for desired reps.
Keep the movement strict: use lighter weight if you must swing the torso or bend the elbows excessively, and breathe out on the lift and in on the lower.
exercises.description: A bent-over dumbbell raise that isolates the rear (posterior) deltoids by lifting the arms out to the sides while the torso is hinged forward. It emphasizes shoulder horizontal abduction and scapular retraction with a controlled tempo.

exercises.name: Cable Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Set the pulleys to the lowest position and attach single D-handles; select an appropriate weight on both sides.
instructions: Set the pulleys to the lowest position and attach single D-handles; select an appropriate weight on both sides.

Stand centered between the two stations with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and a slight bend in the knees; grasp a handle in each hand with a neutral (thumb-up) grip, arms hanging down at your sides.
Cross the cables in front of you if the setup requires it so there is slight tension at the start; keep a small bend in the elbows throughout the exercise.
Brace your core and keep your torso upright (no leaning or swinging). Initiate the movement by lifting the arms out to the sides (lead with the elbows) until your hands reach about shoulder height.
Pause briefly at the top with shoulders down and back (do not shrug), then slowly lower the handles back to the start under control.
Repeat for the desired reps, exhaling as you lift and inhaling as you lower; avoid using momentum or excessive torso tilt to complete reps.
exercises.description: A standing cable lateral raise performed from low pulleys that isolates the lateral deltoid by abducting the arms out to the sides to shoulder height while keeping the torso stable. The cable setup provides constant tension through the movement for controlled concentric and eccentric phases.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Overhead Press

instructions: Stand tall with feet about shoulder-width apart, knees slightly soft (not locked).
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height with palms facing forward or slightly neutral and elbows roughly under the wrists.
Brace your core and squeeze your glutes to maintain a neutral spine and prevent lower-back arching.
Exhale and press both dumbbells straight overhead in a controlled path until your arms are nearly fully extended (avoid jamming the elbows).
Keep the wrists stacked over the elbows and the chin tucked slightly; do not overextend the neck or lower back at the top.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining tension in the delts and scapular stabilizers.
Inhale and lower the dumbbells slowly and under control back to the starting shoulder-height position.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, maintaining core tension and full control on both ascent and descent.
exercises.description: A standing dumbbell overhead press that primarily works the delts by pressing weights from shoulder height to full arm extension overhead, while the triceps and upper-back muscles stabilize and assist.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent and core braced (standing position).
instructions: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent and core braced (standing position).
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with a neutral grip (palms facing your body) and wrists straight.
Keep a small bend in the elbows throughout the movement and maintain a neutral spine and chest up.
Exhale and raise both arms out to the sides by lifting at the shoulders, leading with the elbows so the hands finish roughly at shoulder height (arms nearly parallel to the floor).
Avoid shrugging the shoulders up toward the ears; keep the neck long and scapulae stable to minimize excessive trap involvement.
Pause briefly (0–1 second) at the top while maintaining tension in the delts.
Inhale and lower the dumbbells slowly and controlled back to the starting position at your sides.
Repeat for desired reps, keeping movement smooth and avoiding momentum or swinging; use lighter weight if form breaks down.
exercises.description: Standing dumbbell lateral raise works the lateral delts by lifting the arms out to the sides to about shoulder height, with a slight elbow bend and controlled motion. It improves shoulder width and isolates the middle shoulder fibers while the traps and forearms assist.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press

Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.

exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Raise
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with arms fully extended down at your sides and palms facing inward; keep a slight bend in the elbows.
Brace your core, keep your chest up and neck neutral, and avoid arching your lower back.
Exhale and lift both dumbbells upward and slightly forward in a controlled arc until your hands reach about shoulder height (arms roughly parallel to the floor).
Pause briefly at the top, maintaining tension in the delts and avoiding momentum or swinging.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting hanging position under control.
Perform the desired repetitions, keeping movement smooth and avoiding use of torso momentum; reduce weight if you need to swing to complete reps.
exercises.description: Seated on an incline bench, this variation of the dumbbell raise emphasizes the front portion of the shoulder (anterior delts) by lifting the weights from a hanging position up to about shoulder height. The incline angle shifts some load onto the chest (pectorals) while isolating the delts and minimizing torso swing.

exercises.name: Band Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Stand upright with feet together or hip-width apart, step on the middle of the band to anchor it securely under both feet.

Hold a handle in each hand with palms facing your body and arms hanging at your sides; keep a slight bend in the elbows.

Pause briefly at the top to feel the delts, then inhale and slowly lower the arms back to the starting position under control.

exercises.name: Cable Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single handles.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single handles. Stand centered between the two towers and grasp each handle with the opposite hand so the cables cross in front of you (or hold same-side handles if preferred).
Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart, knees soft, and core braced (neutral spine). Keep a slight bend in the elbows and wrists neutral.
Start with the handles low by your thighs; palms can face inward or slightly forward. Maintain a small forward lean from the hips if comfortable to allow a natural arc.
Keeping the elbows slightly bent, exhale and raise both arms out to the sides in a smooth arc until your hands reach approximately shoulder height. Lead the movement with the elbow, not the wrist.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining tension in the delts; avoid shrugging the shoulders upward toward your ears.
Inhale and lower the handles slowly and under control back to the starting position, keeping tension on the cables throughout the descent.
Repeat for the desired number of reps. Use a lighter weight if you cannot maintain strict form or if you begin using torso momentum.
exercises.description: A standing cable lateral raise performed from low pulleys to isolate the lateral head of the delts; cables provide constant tension while you lift the arms out to the sides to shoulder height. The movement is done with a slight elbow bend and controlled tempo to minimize momentum.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bench Seated Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and knees bent (seated position); keep your chest up and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and knees bent (seated position); keep your chest up and core braced.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height with elbows bent about 90 degrees and palms facing forward or slightly inward (neutral grip); keep wrists stacked over forearms.
Maintain a neutral spine and avoid excessive lower-back arching by tightening your abs and keeping the ribcage down.
Exhale and press both dumbbells upward in a controlled path until your arms are nearly straight overhead but not fully locked out.
Pause briefly at the top with shoulders stabilized (do not shrug up toward your ears), then inhale and lower the dumbbells slowly back to shoulder height.
Keep the movement controlled, elbows tracking under the wrists, and avoid letting the dumbbells drift too far forward or backward.
Perform the desired number of repetitions, focusing on steady tempo and maintaining an upright seated position throughout.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell press performed from a bench, pressing dumbbells overhead to work the shoulder muscles. The movement emphasizes the delts while also engaging the triceps and upper chest for stabilization and extension.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Upright Row
exercises.notes: Start standing upright (not kneeling or prone) with feet hip-width apart and a slight bend in the knees; hold a dumbbell in each hand with a pronated (palms facing body/slightly toward you) grip, arms extended at your sides.
instructions: Start standing upright (not kneeling or prone) with feet hip-width apart and a slight bend in the knees; hold a dumbbell in each hand with a pronated (palms facing body/slightly toward you) grip, arms extended at your sides.
Brace your core, pull your shoulder blades slightly down and back, and keep your chest up and torso vertical throughout the movement.
Initiate the lift by driving your elbows upward and outward, keeping the dumbbells close to the body so they travel in a straight line near the front of your thighs to chest level.

Continue pulling until your elbows reach about shoulder height or slightly above, with elbows higher than your wrists; avoid lifting the dumbbells higher than shoulder level to reduce shoulder impingement risk.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining tension in the delts and traps, then slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position under control.
Keep the wrists neutral (avoid excessive bending), avoid excessive torso swing or leaning back, and do not use momentum—focus on lifting with the shoulders and upper back.
Choose a weight that allows you to perform the set with good form for the desired reps; if you feel excessive shrugging or shoulder pain, reduce weight or limit range of motion.
exercises.description: A standing dumbbell movement where you pull weights up along the front of your body, driving the elbows upward to shoulder level to emphasize the delts and upper traps. Performed with a close grip and elbows leading, it also engages the biceps and forearms for stability.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

exercises.name: Cable Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single handles.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single handles. Stand centered between the two towers and grasp each handle with the opposite hand so the cables cross in front of you (or hold same-side handles if preferred).
Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart, knees soft, and core braced (neutral spine). Keep a slight bend in the elbows and wrists neutral.
Start with the handles low by your thighs; palms can face inward or slightly forward. Maintain a small forward lean from the hips if comfortable to allow a natural arc.
Keeping the elbows slightly bent, exhale and raise both arms out to the sides in a smooth arc until your hands reach approximately shoulder height. Lead the movement with the elbow, not the wrist.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining tension in the delts; avoid shrugging the shoulders upward toward your ears.
Inhale and lower the handles slowly and under control back to the starting position, keeping tension on the cables throughout the descent.
Repeat for the desired number of reps. Use a lighter weight if you cannot maintain strict form or if you begin using torso momentum.
exercises.description: A standing cable lateral raise performed from low pulleys to isolate the lateral head of the delts; cables provide constant tension while you lift the arms out to the sides to shoulder height. The movement is done with a slight elbow bend and controlled tempo to minimize momentum.

exercises.name: Dumbbell One Arm Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Stand upright with feet about hip-to-shoulder width, knees soft, and a neutral spine; hold a dumbbell in the working hand with the other hand resting on your hip or at your side for balance.
instructions: Stand upright with feet about hip-to-shoulder width, knees soft, and a neutral spine; hold a dumbbell in the working hand with the other hand resting on your hip or at your side for balance.
Start with the working arm hanging at your side, palm facing your body and a slight bend in the elbow (avoid locking the joint).
Brace your core and keep the torso still — avoid leaning or swinging the body to lift the weight.
Exhale and raise the dumbbell out to the side in a controlled arc, keeping the elbow slightly bent and the wrist neutral, until the upper arm is approximately parallel to the floor (shoulder height).
Keep the shoulder depressed (do not shrug) and lead the motion with the elbow rather than the hand; avoid rotating the torso.
Pause briefly at the top, then inhale and lower the dumbbell slowly back to the starting position under control.
Perform the desired number of repetitions, then switch arms. Choose a light-to-moderate weight to maintain strict form and avoid momentum.
exercises.description: A single-arm standing dumbbell lateral raise that isolates the lateral deltoid to widen and strengthen the shoulder; the movement lifts the arm out to the side to about shoulder height while the rest of the body remains stable.

exercises.name: Cable Incline Y Raise Wrist Straps with Back Support
exercises.notes: Set a bench to an incline (about 30–45°) and position it so your back is fully supported against the pad.
instructions: Set a bench to an incline (about 30–45°) and position it so your back is fully supported against the pad.
Attach wrist straps to low pulley handles (both sides) and set a light–moderate weight so you can control the entire range.
Sit with feet flat on the floor, chest against the bench, and grasp the wrist straps so your arms hang down in front of you with a slight bend in the elbows; palms should face each other.
Retract and depress your shoulder blades slightly to stabilize the scapulae and maintain a neutral spine throughout the set.
Exhale and raise your arms up and out in a diagonal plane (about 30–45° forward of the frontal plane) to form a Y with your body; lead with the thumbs and keep elbows soft.
Lift until your arms are roughly in line with your head/ears (or you feel a solid contraction in the delts/traps), squeezing the upper back at the top.
Inhale and lower the arms slowly and under control back to the start position, resisting the cable’s pull.

Perform the desired reps while avoiding momentum, excessive lumbar extension, or letting the shoulders excessively shrug; keep the back supported by the bench and use wrist straps to minimize grip fatigue.
exercises.description: Seated on an incline bench with back support, you use low-set cables and wrist straps to lift the arms into a Y shape overhead, emphasizing the shoulder delts and the upper back/trap region. The movement is performed with a controlled lift and slow lowering to isolate the posterior/lateral delts and scapular stabilizers.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.

exercises.name: Cable Front Raise
exercises.notes: Step 1: Attach a straight bar or single handle to the low pulley and select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Step 1: Attach a straight bar or single handle to the low pulley and select an appropriate weight.
Step 2: Stand facing the cable machine with feet hip-width apart, knees soft, and core braced (standing upright).
Step 3: Grasp the bar with an overhand grip (palms down) about shoulder-width, arms extended down in front with a slight bend at the elbows.
Step 4: Maintain a neutral spine and keep shoulders back and down; avoid leaning or using momentum.
Step 5: Exhale and raise the bar straight forward and up with the arms (leading with the elbows) until your hands reach about shoulder height.
Step 6: Pause briefly at the top while keeping tension on the cable and the core engaged.
Step 7: Inhale and slowly lower the bar back to the starting position under control, keeping the slight elbow bend throughout.
Step 8: Repeat for the desired reps; avoid swinging the torso or using excessive weight to maintain strict delt activation.
exercises.description: A standing cable front raise performed from a low pulley that lifts the arms straight in front of the body to shoulder height, primarily working the front portion of the shoulder. It uses a controlled movement with a slight elbow bend to isolate the anterior delts.

exercises.name: Cable Rear Delt Row (with rope)
exercises.notes: Set a cable machine to a high pulley and attach a rope handle.
instructions: Set a cable machine to a high pulley and attach a rope handle. Stand facing the machine about 2–3 feet away.
Grip the rope with both hands using a neutral or thumbs-up grip so the rope ends can separate; step back until there is tension in the cable.
Stand tall with feet shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent and core braced; keep a small forward lean from the hips if needed for balance but maintain a neutral spine.
Start with your arms extended toward the pulley and shoulders relaxed. Keep the elbows slightly below shoulder height on the start.
Pull the rope toward your upper chest/face by driving your elbows out and back (elbows high and flared), externally rotating the shoulders so the rope ends spread apart at the finish.
Squeeze the rear delts and upper back (scapulae) at the top of the movement for 1–2 seconds, keeping the torso stable and avoiding excessive trunk movement.
Slowly return the rope to the start under control, maintaining tension on the cable throughout the eccentric phase.
Repeat for desired reps. Use lighter weight and strict form to maximize rear delt activation and minimize use of the lats and lower back.
exercises.description: A cable exercise using a rope attachment to isolate the rear (posterior) deltoids by pulling the rope toward the face with elbows high and flared, emphasizing scapular retraction and external rotation. Performed standing with a high pulley to target the rear shoulders and upper back.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Cuban Press (version 2)
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees soft and core braced; hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with a neutral grip (palms facing your body).
instructions: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees soft and core braced; hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with a neutral grip (palms facing your body).
Begin the movement by driving your elbows up and out in a controlled upright-row motion, keeping the dumbbells close to the body until your elbows reach roughly shoulder height.

With elbows elevated, externally rotate at the shoulders so your forearms move forward and the dumbbells are brought in front of your face/chest area (upper arms should be roughly parallel to the floor).
From that externally rotated position, press the dumbbells straight overhead until your arms are fully extended, keeping the core tight and avoiding excessive back arch.

Lower the dumbbells back down to the externally rotated position in a controlled manner, then rotate the forearms back to the upright-row position and lower the weights to the starting position at your sides.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, using a weight that allows smooth, controlled rotation and full overhead lockout without shrugging the shoulders.
exercises.description: A standing compound shoulder movement that combines an upright row, external rotation, and overhead press to build deltoid strength and shoulder stability while recruiting the traps and triceps.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.

Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

exercises.notes: Stand upright with feet together or hip-width apart, step on the middle of the band to anchor it securely under both feet.
instructions: Stand upright with feet together or hip-width apart, step on the middle of the band to anchor it securely under both feet.
Hold a handle in each hand with palms facing your body and arms hanging at your sides; keep a slight bend in the elbows.
Brace your abs and keep your torso tall and still; avoid leaning or using momentum.
Exhale and lift both arms out to the sides leading with the elbows until your hands reach shoulder height (arms roughly parallel to the floor).
Keep wrists neutral and maintain the slight elbow bend throughout the movement; do not shrug the shoulders upward.
Pause briefly at the top to feel the delts, then inhale and slowly lower the arms back to the starting position under control.
Repeat for desired reps, maintaining smooth, controlled movement and stable posture on every repetition.

instructions: Sit upright on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and knees bent (seated position); keep your chest up and core braced.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height with elbows bent about 90 degrees and palms facing forward or slightly inward (neutral grip); keep wrists stacked over forearms.
Maintain a neutral spine and avoid excessive lower-back arching by tightening your abs and keeping the ribcage down.
Exhale and press both dumbbells upward in a controlled path until your arms are nearly straight overhead but not fully locked out.
Pause briefly at the top with shoulders stabilized (do not shrug up toward your ears), then inhale and lower the dumbbells slowly back to shoulder height.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Split Stance Single Arm Overhead Press
exercises.notes: Assume a split stance: place one foot forward and the other back with the back foot on the toes; knees should be slightly bent and weight distributed mainly on the front heel for stability.
instructions: Assume a split stance: place one foot forward and the other back with the back foot on the toes; knees should be slightly bent and weight distributed mainly on the front heel for stability.
Pick up a dumbbell with the working hand and bring it to the rack position at shoulder height (dumbbell beside the shoulder, elbow tucked, palm facing inward or slightly forward).
Brace your abs and squeeze the glutes to create a stable, upright torso; keep hips squared and avoid rotating toward the pressing side.
Press the dumbbell straight up in a controlled path until the arm is fully extended overhead, exhaling as you press; keep the wrist neutral and the elbow close to the plane of the body.
Pause briefly at the top with the weight over or slightly behind the shoulder line (not forward of the head) and avoid overarching the lower back.
Slowly lower the dumbbell back to the rack position under control while inhaling, maintaining core tension and the split stance balance.
Complete the desired repetitions, then switch legs and repeat on the other side.
exercises.description: A unilateral overhead press performed from a split stance that primarily works the delts while forcing core and lower-body stabilization. The single-arm demand increases shoulder stability and recruits triceps for lockout and leg/glute muscles to maintain balance.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Overhead Press
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet hip-width apart, knees slightly bent, and core braced (standing position).
instructions: Stand tall with feet hip-width apart, knees slightly bent, and core braced (standing position).
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height with palms facing forward or neutral and elbows slightly in front of the body.
Keep the spine neutral and ribcage down to avoid hyperextending the lower back; squeeze the abs throughout.
Exhale and press both dumbbells upward in a controlled path until the arms are nearly fully extended overhead without locking the elbows.
Pause briefly at the top with shoulders stacked over hips and the head neutral (you may slightly move your head back to allow the dumbbells to pass), keeping shoulder blades stable.
Inhale and lower the dumbbells slowly back to the starting shoulder-height position under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, avoiding excessive torso lean or lower-back arching; if needed, use a lighter weight to maintain good form.
exercises.description: A standing dumbbell overhead press is a compound shoulder exercise where you press dumbbells from shoulder height to full arm extension overhead, primarily working the delts while engaging the triceps and upper back for support and stabilization.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Rear Delt Raise
exercises.notes: Stand feet hip-width apart holding a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral (palms facing) grip.
instructions: Stand feet hip-width apart holding a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral (palms facing) grip.
Hinge forward at the hips until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor, keep a neutral spine and knees slightly bent (bent-over position).
Let your arms hang straight down from the shoulders with a slight bend in the elbows; maintain a braced core and avoid rounding the back.
Keeping the elbows slightly bent, raise the dumbbells out to the sides in a wide arc until your upper arms are roughly parallel to the ground (level with the shoulders).
Squeeze the rear delts and shoulder blades together at the top for a brief pause, avoid shrugging the shoulders up toward the ears.
Slowly lower the dumbbells back to the start under control, resisting any momentum; repeat for desired reps.
Keep the movement strict: use lighter weight if you must swing the torso or bend the elbows excessively, and breathe out on the lift and in on the lower.
exercises.description: A bent-over dumbbell raise that isolates the rear (posterior) deltoids by lifting the arms out to the sides while the torso is hinged forward. It emphasizes shoulder horizontal abduction and scapular retraction with a controlled tempo.

exercises.name: Cable Cross over Revers Fly
exercises.notes: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single-handle grips; select a moderate weight that allows strict movement.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single-handle grips; select a moderate weight that allows strict movement.
Stand centered between the two stacks and cross your hands so each hand holds the handle from the opposite side (or hold same-side handles if preferred), feet hip-width apart.
Hinge at the hips until your torso is angled forward about 30–45 degrees, keep a slight bend in the knees, chest up and back flat; adopt a neutral neck position.
Start with your arms hanging down and handles crossed in front of you, elbows soft with about a 10–20° bend; palms can face each other (neutral) or slightly down.

Exhale and initiate the movement by squeezing the shoulder blades together and externally rotating the shoulders, raising your hands out to your sides in a wide arc until your upper arms are roughly level with your shoulders.
Keep the motion controlled and lead with the elbows rather than the hands; avoid arching the lower back or using momentum.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining scapular squeeze, then inhale and slowly return the handles to the start position under control.
Perform reps for the chosen set, maintaining a steady tempo and ensuring the torso stays stable (no rocking) and shoulders do not shrug up toward the ears.
exercises.description: A bent-over cable reverse fly that isolates the rear delts by pulling low-positioned cables out and back in a wide arc, emphasizing scapular retraction and posterior shoulder activation. Performed standing between low pulleys with a hip hinge to target the posterior shoulder and upper back.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.

exercises.notes: Light weight, focus on form and range of motion

warmup.notes: Brief warmup to activate target muscles. Can skip if first working set feels warm.

exercises.name: Barbell Incline Bench Press

exercises.name: Cable Standing Chest Press
exercises.notes: Set the pulleys to chest height and attach single-handle grips to each side.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Fly

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
Grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width, wrists stacked over elbows and thumbs wrapped around the bar.
Retract and depress the shoulder blades (pinch the shoulder blades together) to create a stable chest platform.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar over the chest so it is balanced and steady.
Inhale, lower the bar under control to the mid-chest (sternum) keeping elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso.
Pause briefly with the bar touching or just above the chest while maintaining tightness through the core and legs.
Exhale and drive the bar upward by extending the elbows, pressing the bar back to the start position while keeping wrists neutral.
Avoid excessive elbow flare or bouncing the bar off the chest; maintain a controlled tempo and keep feet planted.
After the set, carefully re-rack the bar by guiding it back onto the bench supports before releasing your grip.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a supine pressing exercise performed on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also working the triceps and anterior delts. The lifter lowers a barbell to the chest and presses it back to full arm extension under control.

exercises.name: Barbell Incline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench at about 30–45 degrees and position it in a rack with safety catches or a spotter available.
instructions: Set an incline bench at about 30–45 degrees and position it in a rack with safety catches or a spotter available.
Lie supine on the incline bench with your head supported, feet flat on the floor, and back slightly arched while maintaining scapular retraction.
Grip the barbell with a pronated grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists stacked over elbows.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar over your upper chest/shoulder line, then stabilize before starting the descent.
Inhale and slowly lower the bar in a controlled path to the upper chest/clavicular area, keeping elbows at roughly a 30–60° angle from the torso (avoid flaring to 90°).
Lightly touch or stop just above the chest, pause briefly without bouncing, then exhale and press the bar upward and slightly back to the start position, fully extending the elbows.
Keep core braced and feet planted throughout to maintain stability and prevent excessive lower back extension.
Carefully re-rack the bar under control or have a spotter assist when finished.
exercises.description: A barbell press performed on an incline bench that emphasizes the upper portion of the chest by pressing the bar upward and slightly toward the clavicle. It also engages the front delts and triceps for pressing and locking out the weight.

exercises.name: Cable Standing Chest Press
exercises.notes: Set the pulleys to chest height and attach single-handle grips to each side.
instructions: Set the pulleys to chest height and attach single-handle grips to each side.
Stand facing away from the machine and grab a handle in each hand with a neutral or palms-down grip; bring the handles to chest level with elbows bent and hands near the sternum.
Step forward into a staggered or shoulder-width stance so one foot is slightly in front of the other; brace your core and keep a slight bend in the knees.
Keep shoulders down and back, chest up, and press both handles forward and inward until your arms are almost fully extended (do not lock the elbows).
Exhale as you press; hold the end position briefly while keeping tension on the cables.
Inhale and slowly control the return of the handles back to the starting position at chest level, allowing the shoulder blades to set but not excessively retract.
Repeat for the desired reps, maintaining a stable torso and avoiding leaning forward or using excessive lower-back arching.
exercises.description: A standing cable press that targets the chest by pressing handles forward from chest level against cable resistance; the movement also engages the anterior delts and triceps while the core stabilizes the torso. Performed facing away from the cable column with a staggered stance for balance.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Fly
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and lower back in a neutral position.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and lower back in a neutral position.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral or slightly pronated grip and extend the arms straight above your chest, palms facing each other.
Keep a slight, fixed bend in the elbows throughout the movement (do not lock the elbows).
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells out to the sides in a wide arc, maintaining the slight elbow bend until you feel a stretch in the chest or your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor.
Keep the shoulder blades stable on the bench (no excessive shrugging) and avoid letting the dumbbells travel behind the plane of your torso.
Pause briefly at the bottom under control, then exhale and bring the dumbbells back up along the same arc, focusing on squeezing the pectorals until the weights meet above the sternum.
Perform each rep with controlled tempo and avoid bouncing or using momentum; select a weight that allows full control and a safe range of motion.
exercises.description: A supine resistance exercise performed with dumbbells that isolates the chest by moving the arms in a wide arc from above the sternum to the sides, producing a deep stretch and concentric squeeze of the pectorals.

exercises.name: Cable Standing Fly
exercises.notes: Set the cable pulleys to a high or slightly above-shoulder position and attach single-handle grips.
instructions: Set the cable pulleys to a high or slightly above-shoulder position and attach single-handle grips.
Stand centered between the towers in a staggered (split) stance or feet shoulder-width apart with a slight forward lean from the hips so you have stable balance; brace your core.
Grasp a handle in each hand with a neutral or palms-down grip and take one step forward until you feel light tension in the cables; keep a small bend in the elbows throughout.
Start with your arms out to the sides roughly at shoulder height (or slightly higher for a high-cable fly) and chest lifted; retract your shoulder blades slightly to protect the joints.
Exhale and bring the handles together in a controlled arc in front of your chest at midline, leading with the elbows and squeezing the pectorals at the finish.
Pause briefly with hands meeting or slightly overlapping, then inhale and slowly resist the cables as you return your arms to the start position using the same arcing path.
Keep the movement smooth and avoid locking the elbows, excessive torso rotation, or shrugging the shoulders; maintain core tension for stability.
exercises.description: Standing cable fly performed with the pulleys set high to mid-high engages the chest by bringing the arms together in a wide arc; the shoulders and core stabilize the movement. It emphasizes the pectoral fibers with controlled concentric adduction and slow eccentric return.

exercises.name: Cable Bent Over Single Arm Crossover
exercises.notes: Set a single handle on a cable pulley at about shoulder to chest height and select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Set a single handle on a cable pulley at about shoulder to chest height and select an appropriate weight.

Stand to the side of the pulley with a staggered or shoulder-width stance and hinge forward at the hips until your torso is roughly 30–45° from vertical (slight bend in knees); maintain a neutral spine and brace your abs.
Grab the handle with the hand farthest from the machine, arm extended out to the side and palm facing down or slightly inward; allow a soft bend in the elbow.

Keep your shoulder down and stable, then exhale and pull the handle in a horizontal arc across your body toward the center of your chest, leading with the elbow and squeezing the pectorals at the end range.
Pause briefly with the chest contracted, keeping the torso still (avoid rotating the ribs or shoulders), then inhale and slowly reverse the motion to return the hand to the start under control.
Perform the desired reps, then switch sides and repeat. Maintain wrist neutrality and controlled tempo throughout.
exercises.description: A single-arm cable movement performed from a bent-over stance that sweeps the working arm across the body to load and compress the chest fibers, emphasizing the inner and upper pectoral region while the torso is stabilized.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.
instructions: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.

Lie supine on the bench with your head, upper back and glutes in contact with the bench, feet flat on the floor and a small natural arch in the lower back; retract and depress your shoulder blades to create a stable chest platform.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists should be stacked over the elbows and forearms vertical when the bar is over the chest.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar out over the chest while keeping the scapulae stable; lock the elbows briefly before starting the set.
Inhale and lower the bar in a controlled manner to the mid-chest/sternum (around nipple level), keeping the elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso to protect the shoulders.
Allow the bar to touch or come very close to the chest without bouncing; maintain tension in the pectorals and keep the feet and glutes braced.
Exhale and press the bar upward explosively while keeping the bar path slightly arced toward the hips until the arms are fully extended but not hyperextended.
Pause briefly at the top with the shoulders stable, then begin the next rep. Maintain consistent form and breathing throughout the set.
Use a spotter or safety pins in the rack for heavy sets, and avoid flaring the elbows excessively or letting the lower back lift off the bench.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a compound pressing movement performed lying supine on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and anterior delts. It involves lowering a loaded barbell to the chest and pressing it back up to full arm extension with controlled technique.

exercises.name: Barbell Incline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set an adjustable bench to a 30-45° incline and position it inside a power rack or with a spotter and place the barbell on the rack at a height you can reach while seated.
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to a 30-45° incline and position it inside a power rack or with a spotter and place the barbell on the rack at a height you can reach while seated.
Sit with your back and head firmly against the bench, feet flat on the floor, and brace your core (tighten abs and glutes) to maintain a stable, slight arch in the lower back.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists stacked over elbows and shoulders pulled down and back.
Unrack the bar by straightening your arms and moving the bar directly above your upper chest/shoulder line; have a spotter if needed.
Inhale, bend the elbows, and lower the bar under control to the upper chest/clavicle area (touch point just below the top of the sternum) keeping elbows at roughly 30–60° from the torso.
Pause briefly at the bottom without bouncing, ensuring the forearms remain vertical and the core stays braced.
Exhale and press the bar back up in a slightly backward arc until the arms are fully extended but not locked aggressively at the elbows.
Repeat for the desired repetitions, then carefully re-rack the bar on the uprights with control or have the spotter assist.
exercises.description: The barbell incline bench press is a compound pressing movement performed on an inclined bench that emphasizes the upper portion of the pectorals while also engaging the anterior delts and triceps. It is done from a supine position on an incline bench, pressing a barbell vertically from chest to full arm extension.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie back so you are supine on the bench, feet flat on the floor, knees bent, and maintain a neutral spine with a slight natural arch; retract and squeeze your shoulder blades together.
instructions: Sit on the edge of a flat bench with a dumbbell resting on each thigh.
Lie back so you are supine on the bench, feet flat on the floor, knees bent, and maintain a neutral spine with a slight natural arch; retract and squeeze your shoulder blades together.
Use your legs and core to help bring the dumbbells up to chest height so your upper arms are about 45 degrees from your torso and palms are facing forward (or slightly inward if preferred).

Inhale, then slowly lower the dumbbells in a controlled manner until your elbows are roughly level with the bench or just below chest level, keeping wrists neutral and elbows tracking slightly tucked.

Pause briefly at the bottom without relaxing the shoulder blade position, then exhale and press the dumbbells upward and slightly together until your arms are almost fully extended — avoid locking the elbows.
Control the descent back to the starting position and repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
To finish the set, bring the dumbbells safely to your knees, sit up, and set them down or have a spotter assist if using heavy weight.
exercises.description: The dumbbell bench press is a supine pressing movement performed on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and delts for elbow and shoulder extension. Dumbbells allow a greater range of motion and require more stabilization from the forearms and serratus anterior.

exercises.name: Cable Standing Chest Press
exercises.notes: Set the pulleys to chest height and attach single-handle grips to each side.
instructions: Set the pulleys to chest height and attach single-handle grips to each side.
Stand facing away from the machine and grab a handle in each hand with a neutral or palms-down grip; bring the handles to chest level with elbows bent and hands near the sternum.
Step forward into a staggered or shoulder-width stance so one foot is slightly in front of the other; brace your core and keep a slight bend in the knees.
Keep shoulders down and back, chest up, and press both handles forward and inward until your arms are almost fully extended (do not lock the elbows).
Exhale as you press; hold the end position briefly while keeping tension on the cables.
Inhale and slowly control the return of the handles back to the starting position at chest level, allowing the shoulder blades to set but not excessively retract.
Repeat for the desired reps, maintaining a stable torso and avoiding leaning forward or using excessive lower-back arching.
exercises.description: A standing cable press that targets the chest by pressing handles forward from chest level against cable resistance; the movement also engages the anterior delts and triceps while the core stabilizes the torso. Performed facing away from the cable column with a staggered stance for balance.

exercises.name: Cable Incline Fly
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench between two cable towers (about 30–45° incline).
instructions: Set an incline bench between two cable towers (about 30–45° incline). Attach single D‑handles and set the pulleys to low or just below chest height so the cables travel upward across your chest.
Sit on the bench and lie back so your head rests on the pad; grip one handle in each hand with a neutral or palms‑facing posture and feet flat on the floor.
Retract your shoulder blades and brace your core so your back stays pressed into the bench (inclined supine position). Keep a slight, fixed bend in your elbows throughout the exercise.
Start with your arms out to the sides in line with your chest (open position) and inhale, then exhale as you bring the handles together in a wide arc toward the centerline of your upper chest.
Pause briefly at the peak contraction, focusing on squeezing the pectorals and keeping the elbows slightly bent and stable (avoid driving motion from the wrists or excessive shoulder elevation).
Slowly reverse the movement on the inhale, controlling the cables as you return to the start position until you feel a stretch in the chest without losing scapular retraction.
Repeat for the desired reps, maintaining core engagement and avoiding excessive momentum or arching of the lower back.
exercises.description: Lying on an incline bench placed between two cable towers, you perform a bilateral fly motion with a slight elbow bend to concentrically bring the handles together and target the upper chest. The movement emphasizes a horizontal adduction arc while the core stabilizes the torso.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Fly
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and lower back in a neutral position.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and lower back in a neutral position.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral or slightly pronated grip and extend the arms straight above your chest, palms facing each other.
Keep a slight, fixed bend in the elbows throughout the movement (do not lock the elbows).
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells out to the sides in a wide arc, maintaining the slight elbow bend until you feel a stretch in the chest or your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor.
Keep the shoulder blades stable on the bench (no excessive shrugging) and avoid letting the dumbbells travel behind the plane of your torso.
Pause briefly at the bottom under control, then exhale and bring the dumbbells back up along the same arc, focusing on squeezing the pectorals until the weights meet above the sternum.
Perform each rep with controlled tempo and avoid bouncing or using momentum; select a weight that allows full control and a safe range of motion.
exercises.description: A supine resistance exercise performed with dumbbells that isolates the chest by moving the arms in a wide arc from above the sternum to the sides, producing a deep stretch and concentric squeeze of the pectorals.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.
